coredata.dc:title: A glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dots synthesized by a facile post-modification strategy for a sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor of mercury ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Qianqian
Mao, Haichen
Li, Jianjun
Hua, Jianli
Wang, Jizhong
Yang, Ran
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have developed a facile method for the synthesis of glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Gly-GQDs) through post-modification of graphene quantum dots with Gly under alkaline conditions. The as-synthesized Gly-GQDs exhibit an excellent blue emission at 444 nm, independent of excitation, as well as a high quantum yield (QY) of 35.7%. The Gly-GQDs have a narrow size distribution with an average size of 5.9 nm. Moreover, the as-prepared Gly-GQDs showed a better selective and sensitive recognition capability towards mercury ion (Hg2+) in aqueous solutions with a low detection limit of 8.3 nM, compared with GQDs and other nitrogen-doped GQDs synthesized through the one-step solvent thermal method. Gly-GQDs are successfully applied for the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples. This work shows a new promising approach for the design and synthesis of desirable GQDs with a given function.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Post-modification
Mercury ions
Fluorescence detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XPS spectra of GQDs and Gly-GQDs; XPS spectra of N 1s (B), C 1s (C) and O 1s (D) of Gly-GQDs.
The quenching effects of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ ions (5 μM of each) towards GQDs (A), Gly-GQDs (B) and NGQDs (C).
(A) Fluorescence decay curves of Gly-GQDs with and without Hg2+. (B) Absorption spectra of Gly-GQDs after Hg2+ quenching and Cys recovery.
(A) Time-dependent fluorescence response of the Gly-GQDs to 5 μM Hg2+. (B) Fluorescence response of the Gly-GQDs in the absence and presence of 5 μM Hg2+ at different pH values.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Gly-GQDs with different concentrations of Hg2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10 and 20 μM). (B) Relationship between F/F0 and Hg2+ concentrations, inset: calibration plot of F/F0 versus low Hg2+ concentrations (0–3 μM).
Fluorescence intensity changes of Gly-GQDs in the presence of different metal ions (Hg2+: 5 μM, other metal ions: 50 μM).

head.ce:title: A glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dots synthesized by a facile post-modification strategy for a sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor of mercury ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Southern Farmland Pollution Prevention and Control, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Hunan Division of GRG Metrology and Test, Changsha 410000, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Southern Farmland Pollution Prevention and Control
Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs
Hunan Division of GRG Metrology and Test

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Southern Farmland Pollution Prevention and Control, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Hunan division of GRG Metrology and Test, Changsha, 410000, Hunan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have developed a facile method for the synthesis of glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Gly-GQDs) through post-modification of graphene quantum dots with Gly under alkaline conditions. The as-synthesized Gly-GQDs exhibit an excellent blue emission at 444 nm, independent of excitation, as well as a high quantum yield (QY) of 35.7%. The Gly-GQDs have a narrow size distribution with an average size of 5.9 nm. Moreover, the as-prepared Gly-GQDs showed a better selective and sensitive recognition capability towards mercury ion (Hg2+) in aqueous solutions with a low detection limit of 8.3 nM, compared with GQDs and other nitrogen-doped GQDs synthesized through the one-step solvent thermal method. Gly-GQDs are successfully applied for the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples. This work shows a new promising approach for the design and synthesis of desirable GQDs with a given function.

In this work, glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Gly-GQDs) through post-modification of graphene quantum dots with Gly under alkaline conditions. Gly-GQDs showed a better selective and sensitive recognition capability towards msercury ion (Hg2+) than GQDs and other nitrogen-doped GQDs synthesized through one-step solvent thermal method. Gly-GQDs are successfully applied for the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+) is considered as one of the most hazardous and toxic cations concerning human health and the environment [1,2]. Low level of mercury intake for a long period will do toxic harm to human. The upper concentration of Hg2+ is permitted to be 10 nM in drinking water, in order to protect the environment and human health [3,4]. Developing facile and rapid sensing systems for the detection of Hg2+ is urgent and necessary, as traditional analytical technologies have shortcomings, for instance, time-consuming pretreatments, relatively expensive instruments, sophisticated operations, or complicated detection processes [5–8]. The fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection have drawn great attention owing to their outstanding merits, such as time-saving, low-cost, excellent selectivity and sensitivity [9–18]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have excellent fluorescence emission properties and show excellent characteristics distinguished from conventional semiconductor quantum dots for low cytotoxicity, good resistance to photobleaching and excellent biocompatibility. Several GQDs have been reported for the determination of Hg2+ [19–23], however, the low quantum yield (QY) and unsatisfactory sensitivity and selectivity of GQDs towards Hg2+ limited their real applications. It was reported that Fe3+ and Cu2+ usually served as the maximum interference ions in Hg2+ detection [24]. So, it is very desirable to propose a facile synthetic strategy to improve QY and specificity of GQDs for Hg2+.

Functionalization is the efficient method to improve the surface state of GQDs, which may bring about higher QY and many other noble properties [25,26]. One-step hydrothermal or solvent thermal method is widely used for the synthesis of the functional GQDs. However, this one-step pyrolytic or solvent thermal process is usually carried out at a relatively high temperature (over 180 °C) for a long period, accompanied by the pyrolysis and polymerization of functional groups [27,28]. In this way, functional molecules are often partly or totally destroyed leading to the loss of their original function. How to overcome the above limitation of the one-step synthesis method is the key to fabricate desirable GQDs with a given function.

To conserve the original function of the functional molecules, in this work, we adopt a two-step strategy to synthesis functionalized GQDs. First, we synthesis GQDs by pyrolyzing citric acid under 200 °C, then, the obtained GQDs reacted with Gly under alkaline condition at 120 °C. Because of the carbamate reaction between GQDs and Gly, Gly was successfully covalent with GQDs and formed the Gly-GQDs. In contrast to the previous work, the experiment can be finished at a relatively low temperature for a short period of reaction time, effectively protecting the functional reagent from fierce pyrolysis. With Gly integrated onto the edges of GQDs framework, the obtained Gly-GQDs exhibit a higher QY of 35.7% than that of GQDs of 6.7%. Additionally, the residues of Gly were successfully functionalized into the Gly-GQDs, resulting in more selective and sensitive to Hg2+ than GQDs. The Gly-GQDs were then used as fluorescent probes to detect Hg2+ sensitively and selectively in real samples. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first attempt to discuss the influence of synthesis method of functionalized GQDs on their properties. This work shows a new promising approach for the design and synthesis of desirable GQDs with a given function.

Citric acid and Gly were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. NaOH, HgCl2 and other metal salts were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent, Co., China. All reagents were analytical reagent grade and used with no purification. Standard stock solutions of Hg2+ and other metal ions were obtained by dissolving the related metal salts in ultrapure water. A set of different concentrations of Hg2+ solutions were prepared by diluting standard stock solutions. Phosphate buffer solutions of various pH were prepared with various ratios of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 in ultrapure water. River water samples were collected from the river around the campus.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Ltd., Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Co., Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were conducted on a TecnaiG2F20 instrument (FEI, Co., USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. FTIR spectrum in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 was recorded on a Magna-IR 170 spectroscope (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. Element analysis was performed on an Elementar Vario EL CUBE analyzer. Fluorescence decay curves were acquired on an FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., England).

GQDs were prepared first according to the literature [19,29]. Briefly, 2.0 g citric acid was added into a round-bottomed flask and then heated at 200 °C for about 30 min. The liquid obtained was then dissolved in 100 mL 10 mg·mL−1 NaOH aqueous solution to prepare the GQDs solution. The prepared GQDs solution was adjusted to pH 8 and stored at a low temperature.

Gly-GQDs were prepared by a facile post-modification strategy in an alkaline aqueous solution. 50 mg of Gly was added into 3 mL GQDs solution under stirring. NaOH was then put into the mixture to adjust the pH to 12. The mixture was heated at 120 °C for 90 min. Cooling to room temperature, a colorless Gly-GQDs solution was obtained. The solution was slowly neutralized with citric acid and dialyzed in ultrapure water to remove extra reagents. Solid Gly-GQDs material was obtained by freeze-drying and remained stable for at least two months.

As a contrast, nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) were prepared by a pyrolysis method with one-step. 0.5 g citric acid and 0.18 g glycine were added into a 20 mL round-bottom flask and heated from room temperature to 200 °C in 3 min. The color of the liquid was changed from colorless to yellow. Then, the temperature was decreased to 150 °C and remained for about 10 min. The color of the liquid turned to orange, and the heating was over. The NGQDs were achieved by dispersing the obtained liquid into 40 mL redistilled water.
The QY of Gly-GQDs and GQDs was calculated by measuring the fluorescence intensity and UV–vis absorption of the sample. 0.1 M quinine sulfate was taken as a reference.

A certain amount of Gly-GQDs was dissolved in pH 6.0 phosphate buffer to provide an aqueous solution. 40 μL of the Gly-GQDs solution was mixed with 860 μL of pH 6.0 phosphate buffer. Then a series of different concentrations of Hg2+ solutions were mixed with the obtained Gly-GQDs solution, the final solution was precisely fixed to 1.0 mL with pH 6.0 phosphate buffer. Fluorescence spectra of the above solutions were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer with excitation/emission at 350/440 nm.

The synthesized Gly-GQDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR and XPS. The TEM image of the Gly-GQDs (Fig. S1A) reveals that the obtained Gly-GQDs have excellent uniformity and their sizes are mainly 4.5 nm to 7.0 nm, with an average size of 5.9 nm (inset in Fig. S1A). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of Gly-GQDs (Fig. S1B) reveals a good crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm, which well matches with the (002) lattice fringes of graphene [30,31].

The FT-IR spectrum of Gly-GQDs in Fig. S2 exhibits distinct absorption at 3430 cm−1 and 3383 cm−1, attributed to stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The CH stretching vibration is located at 2924 cm−1. The high-intensity peaks at 1567 and 1395 cm−1, and the characteristic band at 1410 cm−1 are related to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO−, and aromatic CN stretching, respectively.

XPS measurement was further used to explore the element type and chemical state of synthesized Gly-GQDs. Fig. 1A verifies that carbon and oxygen elements are present in GQDs and Gly-GQDs. Relative to GQDs, the extra nitrogen peak of Gly-GQDs clearly illustrated that Gly had been successfully bound on the GQDs. The peak at 495.9 eV is ascribed to Na 1s, coming from NaOH. Fig. 1B shows the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum of Gly-GQDs. A deconvoluted peak is obtained at 399.4 eV, assigned to the pyrrolic N. The signal of pyrrolic N confirms that a five-membered ring is formed by N bonding with C through the dehydration of the neighboring carboxyl groups and amino groups of Gly. The N 1s peak at 400.4 eV, suggests that nitrogen has been doped into the graphene framework. The nitrogen content measured by element analysis is 3.99%. Peaks at 284.5, 285.3 and 289.9 eV in the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1C), are related to CC, CN and CO bonding, respectively. CO and COC groups give two deconvoluted peaks at 530.9 and 531.9 eV in Fig. 1D, respectively. The above-mentioned XPS results illustrate that Gly have been successfully doped onto the edges of the framework of GQDs by our proposed post-modification strategy.

Typically, the synthesis of functionalized GQDs can be generally classified into one-step methods [28,32] and two-step methods [27,33]. For the one-step method, the carbon source and nitrogen-containing reagent are treated together under hydrothermal or microwave conditions at high temperature. The high temperature could destroy the structure of the functional molecule of the functional group and the purpose of the functionalization is not achieved.

In this work, a two-step hydrothermal treatment of Gly-GQDs with Gly was applied in the fabrication of Gly-GQDs. GQDs were synthesized first by pyrolyzing citric acid and then the functionalization was carried out through carbodiimide reaction.

To obtain the maximum binding efficiency, the reaction conditions including pH, temperature and reaction time were optimized according to the fluorescence QY. As shown in Fig. S3, the QY of Gly-GQDs reached the highest, when the reaction was conducted at 120 °C, pH 12 for 90 min. Element analysis results of Gly-GQDs prepared at different pH and GQDs are listed in Table S1. When the reaction pH changed from pH 8 to 12, the content of nitrogen greatly increased from the value of 0.07% to 3.92%, revealing that Gly-GQDs are more easily fabricated in alkaline conditions which is consistent with the characteristic of the carbodiimide method.

Different kinds of amine compounds were applied in the post-modification of GQDs, including ammonia, primary amines, and secondary amines. The results are shown in Table S2. From the results, we can see that ammonia and primary amines can be successfully applied in the fabrication of functionalized GQDs through the two-step method, and fluorescence quantum yields of the corresponding nitrogen-doped GQDs greatly increased. Our proposed post-modification strategy is suitable to fabricate functionalized GQDs with ammonia and primary amines.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the as-synthesized Gly-GQDs and GQDs are in Fig. S4A. The GQDs (black, dashed line) give a typical absorption at 365 nm. While, the Gly-GQDs show a broad absorption band (black, solid line) centered at 350 nm, with a blue-shift of 15 nm in contrast to the GQDs. The GQDs (blue, dashed line) and the Gly-GQDs (blue, solid line) show maximum emissions at 462 nm and 444 nm, respectively. The blue-shift between the two samples is considered to be caused by the intense electron affinity of nitrogen doped in the Gly-GQDs [34]. The inset of Fig. S4A shows photographs of aqueous solutions and powders of Gly-GQDs (left) and GQDs (right), taken under an excitation of 365 nm. The GQDs exhibit a blue-green color, whereas the Gly-GQDs are bright blue. The aqueous solution of Gly-GQDs is excited from 290 nm to 400 nm, and the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra are plotted in Fig. S4B. Different from most of the reported GQDs, emission of the as-prepared Gly-GQDs does not show obvious shift, as the excitation is changing. The excitation-independent emission of Gly-GQDs is probably attributed to the surface states rather than the core structures of the Gly-GQDs particles, and the excitation-independent emission property of GQDs has been demonstrated in published works [35–37].

In addition, the synthesized Gly-GQDs have a considerably higher emission than the GQDs. The QY of GQDs and Gly-GQDs is measured to be 6.7% and 35.7%. The fluorescence decay curves of GQDs and Gly-GQDs are shown in Fig. S5. Before nitrogen-doping, the fluorescence lifetime is 1.9 ns for GQDs, whereas it increases to 8.8 ns after nitrogen-doping. The fluorescence enhancement of Gly-GQDs probably derives from the regulation of the chemical and electronic properties of the GQDs, induced by nitrogen doping. In other words, a new origin of fluorescence was obtained when nitrogen-doped on the edges of GQDs, improving the application performance of Gly-GQDs.

GQDs have been reported as efficient fluorescent nanomaterials for detecting Hg2+, because they have a specific chelation towards Hg2+ [19,38]. After nitrogen doping, the as-prepared Gly-GQDs exhibit enhanced fluorescence properties on one hand. On the other hand, a stronger chelating ability might be obtained as nitrogen atoms exist on the surface of the Gly-GQDs. Therefore, the as-prepared Gly-GQDs might become a better fluorescent probe for Hg2+ analysis. The quenching effects of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions (5 μM of each) towards GQDs and Gly-GQDs were tested, and the results are given in Fig. 2A and B, respectively. Compared with GQDs, Hg2+ shows a remarkable quenching effect towards Gly-GQDs. Fe3+ and Cu2+ usually serve as the maximum interference ions in the determination of Hg2+ [20,24]. However, nearly no quenching effect was found for both Fe3+ and Cu2+ towards the as-prepared Gly-GQDs, indicating that the Gly-GQDs might have a better sensitive and selective determination of Hg2+. The NGQDs were also applied in the detection of Hg2+, and the result in Fig. 2C shows that glycine-functionalized GQDs synthesized by one-step method almost have no response to Hg2+. Compared with the conventional one-step method, the proposed post-modification strategy is able to conserve the original function of the functional molecules and fabricate desirable GQDs with a given function.

To investigate the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of Gly-GQDs (Fig. 3A) was measured with and without Hg2+. Before or after Hg2+ ions were added into the Gly-GQDs solution, the lifetime was almost the same (~8.8 ns). It means that the quenching process is a static quenching induced by complexation rather than a dynamic quenching induced by collisional deactivation [39]. To further investigate the interaction of Gly-GQDs with Hg2+, absorption spectra of Gly-GQDs after quenching with Hg2+ were explored. As shown in Fig. 3B, the absorption of Gly-GQDs at 350 nm disappears, and a significant absorption at 320 nm is observed, indicating that the Gly-GQDs-Hg2+ complex is formed. It has been reported that cysteine (Cys) can recover the fluorescence of GQDs by releasing the free GQDs through the formation of CysHg2+ complex [19,40]. Herein, the same amount of Cys was introduced into the quenching system. Both the absorption at 350 nm and the fluorescence of Gly-GQDs were recovered, confirming that the Gly-GQDs quenching is also reversible.

To further study the performance of the Gly-GQDs probes for Hg2+ detection, the emission of the Gly-GQDs was measured every minute after the addition of 5 μM Hg2+. The intensity of the Gly-GQDs at 444 nm nearly decreased by 90% in the first 2 min after the addition of Hg2+ and remained constant during the next 10 min (Fig. 4A). This result demonstrates that the fluorescence quenching of Gly-GQDs by Hg2+ is rapid and stable, and the Gly-GQDs can be applied in fast sensing of Hg2+. The fluorescence of Gly-GQDs is varied with pH values of the solution. It is another crucial factor in the Gly-GQDs sensing process. It can be seen from Fig. 4B, the initial PL intensity (without Hg2+) and the quenched PL intensity (with Hg2+) of the Gly-GQDs are both pH-dependent. For pH < 7, the emission of Gly-GQDs is relatively low and changes with the pH value, whereas the emission becomes the most intense and remains unchanged at pH ≥ 7. Therefore, neutral or alkaline conditions are optimized for this detecting system. Hg2+ leads to the fluorescence quenching of Gly-GQDs from low pH to high pH (green columns). The quenching efficiencies (F/F0) are quite different at different pH. For pH > 7, the quenching efficiencies are not satisfactory. It is probably attributed to the hydrolysis of Hg2+ in alkaline solutions, which prevents Hg2+ from attaching to the Gly-GQDs. Based on the obtained results, pH 6 was selected as the optimum pH for the following quenching effect experiments.

The sensitivity of the fluorescent probe was evaluated, and the result is plotted in Fig. 5A. From the curve, we can see that the emission intensity of 444 nm decreases sharply with the gradually increasing concentration of Hg2+ below 3 μM. The linear quenching range of Hg2+ is from 0 to 3 μM, with a satisfactory correlation coefficient of 0.9990 (inset in Fig. 5B). The detecting limit is calculated to be 8.3 nM, much lower than previously reported values [21–23].

Apart from sensitivity, selectivity is also a critical element for the evaluation of fluorescent probes. In the experiment, representative cations, such as Zn2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Pb2+ and so on, were examined (Fig. 6 ). It is noticeable that the examined cations have no effect on the fluorescence of Gly-GQDs. The emission intensity of Gly-GQDs only decreases sharply in the presence of Hg2+. 10-times (50 μM) of other cations were added into the Gly-GQDs solution, and no obvious decrease of emission intensity was observed. The green columns in Fig. 6 display that the coexistence of other cations does not interfere with Hg2+ determination, and the synthesized Gly-GQDs are suitable to detect Hg2+ even in the presence of different metal cations. The prominent selectivity and specificity may be derived from the strong complexation ability of Hg2+ towards nitrogen and carboxylic groups on the edges of the as-prepared Gly-GQDs.

Real water samples, tap water and river water were investigated to explore the practical applications of the Gly-GQDs. Known concentrations of Hg2+ were added into the original testing samples and analyzed. The total content of Hg2+ in the testing samples were achieved from the calibration curve, and then the concentrations in the original samples were calculated. As listed in Table S3, the recoveries of Hg2+ in water samples are 98.61–104.93%, with RSD ≤ 4.41%. The result confirms the accuracy and reliability of the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples by the Gly-GQDs.

In conclusion, we have proposed a facile post-modification strategy for the fabrication of Gly-functionalized GQDs at a relatively low temperature. After nitrogen groups integrated onto the edges of GQDs frameworks, the as-synthesized Gly-GQDs exhibited 1) enhanced fluorescence with a 35.7% QY and excitation-independent blue emission; and 2) stronger specific chelating ability towards Hg2+. The Gly-GQDs were further used to detect Hg2+ selectively and sensitively, with a comparably low detecting limit of 8.3 nM. The sensing system possesses high selectivity and is capable of determining Hg2+ content. This work supplies a potential method for the fabrication of functionalized GQDs with novel properties. Functionalized GQDs can be acquired by synthesizing GQDs at first and then post-modifying at a mild condition, which can effectively prevent the functional groups from being over-pyrolyzed.

Qianqian Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Haichen Mao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Jianjun Li: Data curation. Jianli Hua: Funding acquisition. Jizhong Wang: Funding acquisition. Ran Yang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Zhaohui Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1804136, 21708035), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M620302, 2018T110736), and the 2019 Youth Talents Promotion Project of Henan Province (2019HYTP003).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119090.
